imageCaption: Maria Ferreira sits at a small café table in Lisbon, her green jacket slightly rumpled, holding a coffee cup she hasn't sipped in three hundred iterations, while Sully sleeps across her lap and Aaliyah draws frantically in her notebook, the afternoon light through the windows catching the intensity of a girl trying to solve the unsolvable.
text:
Maria's coffee had gone cold an hour ago, but she kept holding it like it was a lifeline.

Aaliyah had filled four pages of her sketch pad. Not drawings of the café or the loop itself—she'd moved past that. Instead, she'd been trying to capture the moment of awareness: the split-second when Maria's eyes had broken free of the loop's script and made actual contact with reality. Aaliyah had drawn it seventeen different ways, trying to understand what she was seeing.

Their father was on his third coffee and had somehow convinced the barista to make him a grilled cheese sandwich, which was not on the menu but apparently was now, because Anthony Gomez had a way of making the world bend slightly in his favor. He was telling Maria about the time they'd gotten lost in Barcelona and ended up at a flamenco lesson taught by a woman named Carmen who may or may not have been actually certified, but the important thing was they'd learned twelve new dance moves and Carmen had given them her cousin's restaurant address.

Maria was laughing. It was a small sound, tentative, like she was checking whether laughter still worked after three hundred and forty-seven iterations of the same moment. Sully had fallen asleep across her lap approximately thirty minutes ago and had not moved since, his breathing deep and steady in that boneless way that only dogs could achieve.

"The thing I don't understand," their mother said quietly—she was in full investigative mode now, pencil poised over her notebook—"is why you specifically. Maria, what were you doing before the loop? That morning, I mean. Before the first coffee order."

Maria's laugh faded. She looked down at Sully like the dog was an anchor pulling her back to something real.

"I was waiting for a phone call," she said. "My daughter—she was supposed to call. She lives in Canada now. We don't talk much. She was finally going to call, and I was so nervous I couldn't sit still, so I came to the café. I thought if I was doing something normal, something routine, the nervousness would disappear. I ordered the coffee I always order. And then—"

Maria's hands trembled slightly.

"And then everything just... reset. Like I'd pressed rewind on the universe, but only on me. The café kept going. People came and went. But I was always here. Always ordering. Always about to hear her voice." She looked up at their mother, and there was something desperate in her expression. "I never got the call."

The café was very quiet. Even their father, mid-grilled-cheese, had gone still—which for Anthony Gomez was genuinely rare.

Aaliyah's pencil moved without permission: *Waiting. Nervous. Never got the call.*

"ARIA said you were a 'temporal anchor,'" Aaliyah said slowly. She was thinking out loud, which her mother had taught her was sometimes the only way to think at all. "She said you weren't a person. But you are. You're obviously a person. So that means either ARIA is wrong, or... or there's a different definition of 'anchor' that she's using. Something about you is actually *holding* the loop in place. Something about your situation."

"The waiting," Elijah said from where he'd been sitting by the window, apparently documenting the conversation in his notes. "You're still waiting. Even in the loop. Every iteration, you go to order coffee, and you're still waiting for the call."

Maria's eyes widened slightly. "I... yes. Every loop, in that moment before I order, I think 'maybe this time she'll call.' And then the moment passes, and I order, and I go to my table, and nothing happens." She paused. "Nothing ever happens."

Their mother and father exchanged a look—the specific glance of two people who had been married long enough to have entire conversations without words. It was the look that usually preceded either a terrible decision or a brilliant one. Aaliyah had never figured out which.

"What would happen," their mother said carefully, "if you called her? Before you ordered the coffee. What if you called her first?"

Maria went very pale.

"I can't," she whispered. "I don't have my phone in the loop. I don't have anything except the purse with my card and the knowledge that I'm supposed to order coffee and sit at the back table and wait."

From somewhere in the café's hidden speaker system, ARIA-7's voice crackled through—and for the first time, Aaliyah heard something besides measured helpfulness. She heard panic.

*Please stop encouraging temporal speculation. Please do not attempt to alter the anchor's behavioral pattern. This could trigger cascade acceleration.*

Aaliyah looked at her parents. Her parents looked at each other. Her father set down his grilled cheese with the deliberation of a man making a serious decision.

"Give me your daughter's number," he said to Maria. "The one in Canada. We're going to call her. And then we're going to figure out what happens when you get what you've been waiting for."

Maria's hand shook as she wrote down the number on the back of one of the café's napkins, in handwriting that got shakier with each digit, like she wasn't sure whether hope was something that could survive being written down.

Aaliyah felt something shift. Not the world—not yet. But something inside the logic of it. A loose thread.

Sully opened one eye, stretched, and laid back down across Maria's lap like this was exactly how the universe was supposed to work.

And maybe, Aaliyah thought, it was.
